abstract: This article examines how Article 40 of the European Union's Digital Services Act (DSA) reshapes the practical landscape of platform research by establishing new mechanisms for researcher access to platform data. The authors assess what the regulation changes in day-to-day research practice and what challenges and opportunities remain for scholars studying very large online platforms. The piece appears as a contribution to Political Communication discussing the implications of this regulatory shift.
key_claims: Article 40 of the DSA represents a substantive change in the conditions under which platform research can be conducted in the EU.
The new access regime alters both opportunities and constraints for researchers compared to the prior environment of largely voluntary or restricted platform data access.
methods: Conceptual and policy-oriented analysis of Article 40 DSA and its implications for empirical platform research practice.
findings: Detailed findings are not specified in the available abstract.
contributions: Provides researchers in political communication and adjacent fields with an assessment of how DSA Article 40 reconfigures access to platform data.
Bridges regulatory developments and methodological practice in platform studies.
framing: The paper is positioned within ongoing debates in political communication and platform studies about data access, transparency, and the governance of very large online platforms, engaging directly with the EU's emerging digital regulatory framework.
